pattern: Posterior fossa mass in a child

findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a solid, well-marginated mass centered within the cerebellar vermis and the roof of the fourth ventricle [1, 2]. On unenhanced CT, the tumor characteristically appears hyperdense due to its high cellularity, while on MRI it is typically T1 hypointense, T2 iso- to hyperintense, and demonstrates restricted diffusion [2, 3].
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include homogeneous enhancement following contrast administration, although small cysts or areas of necrosis may occasionally be present [3]. There is typically significant mass effect causing anterior displacement and compression of the fourth ventricle, which frequently results in obstructive hydrocephalus [4, 5].
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of a predominantly cystic mass with a distinct enhancing mural nodule to suggest a juvenile pilocytic astrocytoma [6]. Furthermore, there is no extension of a heterogeneous, calcified mass through the foramina of Luschka or Magendie into the cerebellopontine angle, which would suggest an ependymoma [3, 7].
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of a midline, hyperdense, solid vermian mass with restricted diffusion causing obstructive hydrocephalus in a pediatric patient [2-4].

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Medulloblastoma, given the midline location arising from the superior medullary velum, the hyperdensity on CT, and the restricted diffusion [2, 3]. This is a highly malignant primitive neuroectodermal tumor and is the most common pediatric posterior fossa mass [1, 2].
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Juvenile pilocytic astrocytoma (JPA), however this tumor typically arises off-midline within the cerebellar hemispheres [6]. Additionally, JPAs classically present as a strongly cystic lesion with a brightly enhancing mural nodule, rather than a predominantly solid, hyperdense mass [3, 6].
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Ependymoma, given it also frequently presents as a mass filling the fourth ventricle in children and missing it could alter the surgical approach since it arises from the floor rather than the roof of the ventricle [2, 8]. However, ependymomas are generally more heterogeneous, calcify in roughly 50% of cases, and characteristically exhibit 'plastic' growth extending through the ventricular exit foramina [4, 9].

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately assess the degree of mass effect and obstructive hydrocephalus, and to evaluate the entire neuroaxis for cerebrospinal fluid drop metastases prior to surgical intervention [2, 3].
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to assess the chronicity of the ventricular dilatation and verify the rapid growth typical of this highly malignant primitive neuroectodermal tumor [1, 3].
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a contrast-enhanced MRI of the entire brain and whole spine, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely defining the relationship of the tumor to the brainstem and evaluating for subarachnoid seeding or spinal drop metastases [2, 3, 10].
management.mriSequences: I would specify diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) to confirm the highly cellular nature of the tumor, alongside sagittal T1-weighted post-contrast sequences of the entire neuroaxis to meticulously screen for leptomeningeal 'icing-like' (Zuckerguss) enhancement and spinal drop metastases [2, 10].
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Pediatric Neuro-oncology MDT. My report would clearly document the origin of the tumor from the roof of the fourth ventricle, the severity of hydrocephalus, and the presence or absence of leptomeningeal spread to guide urgent neurosurgical resection, ventricular shunting, and adjuvant chemoradiotherapy [1, 2, 11].

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a solid, well-marginated mass centered within the cerebellar vermis and the roof of the fourth ventricle [1, 2]. On unenhanced CT, the tumor characteristically appears hyperdense due to its high cellularity, while on MRI it is typically T1 hypointense, T2 iso- to hyperintense, and demonstrates restricted diffusion [2, 3]. Supporting features include homogeneous enhancement following contrast administration, although small cysts or areas of necrosis may occasionally be present [3]. There is typically significant mass effect causing anterior displacement and compression of the fourth ventricle, which frequently results in obstructive hydrocephalus [4, 5]. Importantly, there is no evidence of a predominantly cystic mass with a distinct enhancing mural nodule to suggest a juvenile pilocytic astrocytoma [6]. Furthermore, there is no extension of a heterogeneous, calcified mass through the foramina of Luschka or Magendie into the cerebellopontine angle, which would suggest an ependymoma [3, 7]. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of a midline, hyperdense, solid vermian mass with restricted diffusion causing obstructive hydrocephalus in a pediatric patient [2-4].

The appearances are most in keeping with Medulloblastoma, given the midline location arising from the superior medullary velum, the hyperdensity on CT, and the restricted diffusion [2, 3]. This is a highly malignant primitive neuroectodermal tumor and is the most common pediatric posterior fossa mass [1, 2]. The principle differential would be Juvenile pilocytic astrocytoma (JPA), however this tumor typically arises off-midline within the cerebellar hemispheres [6]. Additionally, JPAs classically present as a strongly cystic lesion with a brightly enhancing mural nodule, rather than a predominantly solid, hyperdense mass [3, 6]. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Ependymoma, given it also frequently presents as a mass filling the fourth ventricle in children and missing it could alter the surgical approach since it arises from the floor rather than the roof of the ventricle [2, 8]. However, ependymomas are generally more heterogeneous, calcify in roughly 50% of cases, and characteristically exhibit 'plastic' growth extending through the ventricular exit foramina [4, 9].

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately assess the degree of mass effect and obstructive hydrocephalus, and to evaluate the entire neuroaxis for cerebrospinal fluid drop metastases prior to surgical intervention [2, 3]. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to assess the chronicity of the ventricular dilatation and verify the rapid growth typical of this highly malignant primitive neuroectodermal tumor [1, 3]. I would recommend a contrast-enhanced MRI of the entire brain and whole spine, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely defining the relationship of the tumor to the brainstem and evaluating for subarachnoid seeding or spinal drop metastases [2, 3, 10]. I would specify diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) to confirm the highly cellular nature of the tumor, alongside sagittal T1-weighted post-contrast sequences of the entire neuroaxis to meticulously screen for leptomeningeal 'icing-like' (Zuckerguss) enhancement and spinal drop metastases [2, 10]. This should go to the Pediatric Neuro-oncology MDT. My report would clearly document the origin of the tumor from the roof of the fourth ventricle, the severity of hydrocephalus, and the presence or absence of leptomeningeal spread to guide urgent neurosurgical resection, ventricular shunting, and adjuvant chemoradiotherapy [1, 2, 11].
